cataloging_subject.label: Lydgatiana, installments I-V
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The cataloged subject is Henry Noble MacCracken's five-installment scholarly compilation Lydgatiana as encoded by U-M and preserved in CME00109: twenty-six distinct Middle English verse texts plus MacCracken's modern editorial introductions. The poems, their manuscript versions, MacCracken's serial articles, the U-M encoding, and this repository artifact remain distinct layers. The title does not make John Lydgate author of the compilation's anonymous poems.

catalog_summary.title: Lydgatiana
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous authors
catalog_summary.date: Medieval texts chiefly fifteenth century; serial edition 1911-1913
catalog_summary.region: England; individual witnesses and one uncertain northern/Scottish hypothesis are scoped below

titles.value: Twenty-six Middle English poems edited under the title Lydgatiana

agents.name: John Marion
notes: Named by the explicit of the alphabetic Marian poem printed as item V.12; this does not imply responsibility for other items.

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: Recorded only for disputed or inherited attributions and stylistic association, not as author of the whole compilation.

scope.description: All twenty-six medieval items except John Marion's ABC; several items carry rejected or uncertain historical ascriptions
Historical Lydgate ascriptions or inclusion in a Lydgatiana context, especially John Stow's added names in Trinity R.3.21

notes: MacCracken explicitly rejects Stow's authority for the Trinity attributions and presents the two ballades and fourteen religious poems as anonymous works under Lydgatian influence.

scope.description: Electronic encoding of MacCracken's five installments
The twenty-six historical texts, not MacCracken's framing

notes: MacCracken's dating rests primarily on meter and language; manuscript dates are not composition dates.

date_statements.display: first half of the fifteenth century, often placed about 1450

notes: The poem names Agincourt and the 1436 campaigns at Calais and Roxburgh.

date_statements.display: after the Battle of Barnet, 14 April 1471

scope.description: Broad origin of the Middle English contents

notes: MacCracken inferred a northern author or linguistic setting; this remains a hypothesis.
Possible Scottish origin is reported in later discussion but is not established.
The poem concerns the 1471 Battle of Barnet and London political news; this is not a claim of composition place.

scope.description: Rubrics, refrains, headings, and devotional phrases occur within several poems

form_statements.description: A modern prose-framed scholarly compilation whose historical contents are verse

scope.description: Job, Three Kings, Trinity religious texts, and the fourteen short religious poems

subject_statements.label: Biblical Magi and the Three Kings of Cologne
Job as an example of patience

summaries.value: Lydgatiana is Henry Noble MacCracken's five-part gathering of twenty-six Middle English poems that he considered relevant to the study of John Lydgate without treating the collection as a secure Lydgate canon. It ranges from a Life of Job and a fragmentary verse Three Kings of Cologne through courtly complaints, a Life of St Anne, Passion and daily prayers, a Battle of Barnet song, and fourteen short religious poems dominated by Marian devotion. CME00109 retains the serial installments' modern introductions and their medieval texts. Its direct manuscript path crosses fifteen codices; John Marion is named only for item V.12, while the remaining authors are anonymous or disputed.

extent.description: The cataloged historical selection contains twenty-six verse works or fragments grouped under five installment sections: 181, 171, 859, 1,176, and 1,118 historical verse lines respectively, totaling 3,505 L elements in 491 LG elements. The XML has 3,509 L elements overall because four additional quoted verse lines occur in MacCracken's modern prose introductions. It also contains five introduction divisions, 53 P, 80 PB, and 125 NOTE elements; those representation features are not silently added to the medieval-item extent.

content_parts.label: I. The Life of Holy Job
content_parts.summary: The 1911 first installment introduces and prints one twenty-six-stanza Job poem from former Phillipps 8299, now Huntington HM 140.

content_parts.label: The Life of Holy Job
content_parts.summary: A twenty-six-stanza narrative of Job's prosperity, trials, patience, death, and salvation; MacCracken prints it from the unique HM 140 witness.

content_parts.label: II. Two Chaucerian Ballades
content_parts.summary: The 1911 second installment prints two anonymous complaints from Tanner 346 and reports variants from the later Findern copy.

content_parts.label: As ofte as syghes ben in herte trewe
content_parts.summary: A thirteen-stanza lover's complaint with the refrain 'So ofte, and ofter, I sygh for youre sake.'

content_parts.label: Compleynt for Lac of Sight
content_parts.summary: A sixty-eight-line complaint arranged by MacCracken as a triple ballade with envoy, lamenting the beloved's absence.

content_parts.label: III. The Three Kings of Cologne
content_parts.summary: The 1912 third installment introduces and prints the unique, acephalous Middle English verse version from the London Thornton manuscript.

content_parts.label: The Three Kings of Cologne
content_parts.summary: The unique verse retelling of the Magi legend, surviving without roughly its first hundred lines and beginning within the journey narrative; it has 123 rhyme-royal groups in the encoding.

content_parts.label: IV. Unprinted Texts from Trinity R.3.21
content_parts.summary: The 1913 fourth installment prints eight poems from one Trinity manuscript and warns against John Stow's added Lydgate ascriptions.

content_parts.label: Life of St Anne
content_parts.summary: A ninety-four-stanza sacred life beginning with a prologue and transmitted anonymously despite Stow's added Lydgate label.

content_parts.label: Psalms of the Passion
content_parts.summary: A sequence of Passion meditations headed Incipiunt Psalmi Passionis domini.

content_parts.label: A Devout Prayer toward Bed at Night
content_parts.summary: A six-stanza nightly prayer for protection, contrition, and a good end.

content_parts.label: A Devout Prayer at Thy Uprising
content_parts.summary: An eight-stanza morning prayer beginning 'Jesu Lord blessed Thou be.'

content_parts.label: A Lament of Our Lady at the Passion
content_parts.summary: A fifteen-stanza Marian lament at the Cross; the Lydgate attribution added in Stow's hand is not accepted as authorship.

content_parts.label: An Exhortation to Avoid the Seven Sins
content_parts.summary: Ten verse groups exhort against pride, wrath, envy, sloth, gluttony, lust, and avarice.

content_parts.label: A Song on the Battle of Barnet
content_parts.summary: A thirty-one-line political song on the Yorkist victory at Barnet followed by a four-line aphoristic quatrain, represented by two DIMEV records.

content_parts.label: The Birds' Matins
content_parts.summary: A ten-stanza avian devotional dialogue; MacCracken transcribes the first R.3.21 copy, while the manuscript contains a second copy at ff. 246r-v.

locators.value: base copy ff. 196v-197r, canvases 398-399; duplicate ff. 246r-v

content_parts.label: V. Fourteen Short Religious Poems
content_parts.summary: The final 1913 installment gathers fourteen largely anonymous devotional poems from eleven codices, with strong Marian emphasis.

content_parts.label: Hoc Factum Est a Domino
content_parts.summary: A twelve-stanza poem that redirects scholastic and historical questions toward faith, including Agincourt and the 1436 Calais and Roxburgh campaigns.

content_parts.label: A Prayer to Christ's Name
content_parts.summary: A three-stanza prayer to Jesus' name; direct image inspection reconciles MacCracken's provider folio 124v with DIMEV's current folio 146v.

locators.value: provider label f. 124v; DIMEV current f. 146v; canvas 252

content_parts.label: Have Mercy on Me
content_parts.summary: A five-stanza penitential prayer beginning 'Almighty God maker of heaven.'

content_parts.label: Salve Regina
content_parts.summary: A four-stanza Marian prayer beginning 'Salve with all obeisance to God in humblesse.'

content_parts.label: All Hail Mary
content_parts.summary: A Marian praise poem of forty-seven encoded lines.

content_parts.label: Prayers to Mary and the Saints
content_parts.summary: A nine-stanza petition to Mary and named saints; the current DIMEV boundary is broader than MacCracken's printed locator.

content_parts.label: Lenvoy to Mary
content_parts.summary: A six-stanza Marian envoy beginning 'Go little bill and do me recommend.'

content_parts.label: Regina Celi Letare
content_parts.summary: A four-stanza Marian hymn with the Latin opening as refrain and rubric.

content_parts.label: The Five Joys of the Virgin
content_parts.summary: Five rhyme-royal groups on Mary's joys; MacCracken chooses the Cotton version over the poorer Ashmole 59 copy.

content_parts.label: Maria Virgo Assumpta Est
content_parts.summary: A ten-stanza Marian poem with Latin refrain; MacCracken's headnote says Harley 2250, but the contemporary description, DIMEV, and the poem's location support Harley 2251.
notes: Harley 2250 is retained as a documented printed shelfmark error, not modeled as the source witness.

content_parts.label: An Acrostic on MARIA
content_parts.summary: A fifteen-stanza Marian acrostic beginning 'Away faint love full of variance.'

content_parts.label: John Marion's ABC to the Virgin
content_parts.summary: A twenty-two-stanza alphabetic Marian poem explicitly signed by John Marion; a printed folio milestone in CME confuses the shelfmark 168 with a folio number.

content_parts.label: An ABC to the Virgin from a Leyden Manuscript
content_parts.summary: A twenty-seven-stanza rhyme-royal alphabetic hymn to Christ and the Virgin from Leiden Vossius Germ. Gall. Q.9.

content_parts.label: O Flos Pulcherrime
content_parts.summary: A twenty-two-stanza Marian prayer beginning 'Might, wisdom, goodness of the Trinity.'

related_works.title: Book of Job
notes: The poem retells the biblical Job story but is not a transcription or translation of one identified physical biblical exemplar.

related_works.title: Historia trium regum
notes: The verse Three Kings belongs to the English reception of John of Hildesheim's narrative; its exact textual route remains unresolved.

related_works.title: Works of John Lydgate
notes: MacCracken groups these texts for canon and influence study; association is not blanket authorship.

evidence.locator: IDG, TEMPHEAD, TEXT and complete BODY
evidence.summary: Direct evidence for the five-installment structure, twenty-six historical items, titles, introductions, verse, notes, printed pagination, manuscript milestones, and formatting.

evidence.citation: Reproducible XML structural audit of CME00109.xml.
evidence.summary: Confirms 266,734 bytes; five DIV1 installment units; 31 DIV2, nine DIV3, 3,509 L, 491 LG, 53 P, 80 PB, and 125 NOTE elements. Per-item inspection assigns 3,505 L to the twenty-six historical poems and four L to editorial quotations.

evidence.citation: Henry Noble MacCracken, 'Lydgatiana' I-V, Archiv für das Studium der neueren Sprachen und Literaturen 126-127, 129-131 (1911-1913).
evidence.summary: The five immediate source editions establish selection boundaries, manuscript bases, editorial claims, rejected attributions, titles, lineation, and historical framing. Complete scans of all five source volumes are linked in the lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: Digital Index of Middle English Verse, version 1.0, records for the twenty-six CME00109 textual units.
evidence.summary: Versioned evidence for work boundaries, incipits, forms, subjects, witness loci, collateral witnesses, current foliation, and the Harley 2251 correction for Maria Virgo Assumpta Est.

evidence.citation: Linked official catalogs and IIIF manifests for the fifteen direct manuscript witnesses.
evidence.summary: Official and image-level evidence for current shelfmarks, physical objects, exact target loci where cataloged, digital availability, and dual-foliation mappings. Detailed links and local-copy identities are in the lineage manifest.

evidence.citation: Frank Schaer, The Three Kings of Cologne: manuscript tradition and Middle English versions (PhD thesis, University of Adelaide, 1992).
evidence.locator: discussion of the unique verse version and Add MS 31042
evidence.summary: Supports the unique, acephalous verse-work boundary, relation to John of Hildesheim's tradition, approximate dating, and cautious treatment of northern or Scottish origin and Lydgate authorship.

evidence.citation: Archives de littérature du Moyen Âge, 'The life of holy Job,' record 17998.
evidence.summary: Confirms the title, fifteenth-century date, 181-line twenty-six-stanza form, HM 140 locus, incipit, explicit, and MacCracken bibliography.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library, Corpus of Middle English item CME00109.
evidence.summary: Official identity and public presentation of the electronic compilation; exact structural claims are independently grounded in the checked-in XML.

open_questions.question: Which of the anonymous Lydgatiana poems, if any, can be assigned to John Lydgate or to identifiable authors beyond John Marion?
next_steps: Review post-DIMEV attribution scholarship item by item.
Treat Stow's added names, MacCracken's stylistic comparisons, and manuscript rubrics as scoped evidence rather than blanket authorship.
Preserve John Marion's signed item as the only currently confirmed named authorship.

open_questions.question: What exact textual route, date, and region produced the unique verse Three Kings of Cologne?
next_steps: Compare Add MS 31042 directly with Latin and Middle English prose versions.
Reassess the northern and possible Scottish linguistic claims.
Do not convert MacCracken's stylistic proximity to Lydgate into an attribution.

open_questions.question: Can all legacy/current foliation and shelfmark conflicts be confirmed from institutional collation statements?
next_steps: Ask CUL to document the Findern old/current foliation concordance used by CUDL and DIMEV.
Confirm Arundel 249 and Cotton Caligula A II boundaries against images.
Treat Harley 2250 in MacCracken's item V.10 as a printed error unless contrary primary evidence appears.
Keep the spurious Arundel '168v' milestone separate from the true f. 1 locus.

open_questions.question: Can complete reusable facsimiles be obtained for the British Library, Leiden, and only-partially-imaged Bodleian witnesses?
next_steps: Request complete whole-object reproductions by shelfmark, separately identifying work folios.
Ask for written repository-display, scholarly-publication, and commercial print-on-demand terms.
Do not treat a catalog page or selected-image IIIF object as a complete manuscript facsimile.

notes: The five parent parts model MacCracken's serial editorial installments; their twenty-six children model the historical textual units selected within them.
The historical selection has 3,505 verse lines. The XML's four additional L elements are quotations embedded in modern editorial introductions and are excluded from historical-work extent.

The Battle of Barnet item contains a song plus a following four-line aphoristic quatrain represented by separate DIMEV records; they remain one MacCracken numbered item with an internal two-work caveat.
Complete-facsimile cache status and exact download links are recorded in the linked lineage manifest, not inferred from catalog availability.
